In the {{jalink|Meiji period}}, the "{{jalink|Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|Hakusan Gongen shrine}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} 6 ({{jalink|1873|Q7757}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}} 14 ({{jalink|2002|Q1987}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{jalink|Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511}} on the {{jalink|Chūō Main Line|Q1078110}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{jalink|tunnel|Q44377}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. {{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|lt=Ginpeisha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=Ginpeisha}} / {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920}} is regarded as the place where {{ill||lt=Amaterasu|UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill||lt=Meiji period|UNKNOWN}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|UNKNOWN}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=UNKNOWN}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=UNKNOWN}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なかかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:岐阜県の旧村社]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

[[JA:中川神社 (中津川市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11364920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Nakagawa Shrine | 画像=[[File:中川神社01.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052}}4-816-1 Nakatsugawa | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}} | 社格={{ill|lt=Ginpeisha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=Ginpeisha}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なかかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:岐阜県の旧村社]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

画像=[[File:中川神社01.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}4-816-1 Nakatsugawa | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}}
{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}}
{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} | 社格={{ill|lt=Ginpeisha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=Ginpeisha|1=Kinpei-sha}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924|1=Ena District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa|1=Nakatsu River}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なかかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:岐阜県の旧村社]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924|1=Ena District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa|1=Nakatsu River}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:なかかわしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:岐阜県の旧村社]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]][[Category:qq]]

It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. 画像=[[File:中川神社01.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}4-816-1 Nakatsugawa | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}}
{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}}
{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} | 社格={{ill|lt=Ginpeisha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=Ginpeisha|1=Kinpei-sha}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924|1=Ena District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa|1=Nakatsu River}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]][[Category:qq]]

The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]]

画像=[[File:中川神社01.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}4-816-1 Nakatsugawa | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}}
{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}}
{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} | 社格={{ill|lt=Ginpeisha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=Ginpeisha|1=Kinpei-sha}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924|1=Ena District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa|1=Nakatsu River}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:中津川市の神社]]

{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}}
{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} | 社格={{ill|lt=Ginpeisha|Q119929592|WD=Q119929592|lt=Ginpeisha|1=Kinpei-sha}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|WD=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|WD=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|WD=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kukurihime}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|WD=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|WD=Q3030924|1=Ena District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|WD=Q864156|1=Mino Province}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|WD=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|WD=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|WD=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|WD=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|WD=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|WD=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa|1=Nakatsu River}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|WD=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|WD=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|WD=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|WD=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|WD=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|WD=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|WD=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|WD=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|WD=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|WD=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|WD=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|WD=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|WD=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|WD=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|WD=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nakatsugawa]]

'岐阜県中津川市北野町816-1', 'language': 'ja'} {{commons category|Category:Nakagawa-jinja (Nakatsugawa)}} [[JA:中川神社 (中津川市)]] {{wikidata link|Q11364920}} == Japanese content == {{神社 | 名称=Nakagawa Shrine | 画像=[[File:中川神社01.jpg|270px]]
Haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|qid=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|qid=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}4-816-1 Nakatsugawa | 位置= {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|qid=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kikurihime}}
{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}}
{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|qid=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} | 社格={{ill|lt=Ginpeisha|Q119929592|qid=Q119929592|lt=Ginpeisha|1=Kinpei-sha}} / {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} (small)
former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|qid=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|qid=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|qid=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|qid=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kikurihime}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|qid=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|qid=Q3030924|1=Ena District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|qid=Q864156|1=Mino Province}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|qid=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|qid=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|qid=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|qid=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|qid=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa|1=Nakatsu River}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|qid=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|qid=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|qid=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|qid=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|qid=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|qid=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|qid=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|qid=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|qid=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|qid=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|qid=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|qid=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|qid=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|qid=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nakatsugawa]]

former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | 創建=Unknown | 本殿=sangen-sha {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} | 例祭=2nd Sunday of April | 神事= | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Gifu|label=Nakagawa Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=230}} }} '''Nakagawa Shrine''' (なかがわじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa|Q844052|qid=Q844052|1=Nakatsugawa}}, {{ill|lt=Gifu Prefecture|Q131277|qid=Q131277|1=Gifu Prefecture}}. Its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|qid=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. === Enshrined deities === *{{ill|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Kami|Q11619891|qid=Q11619891|lt=Kukuri-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Kikurihime}} *{{ill|lt=Izanami|Q682306|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami-no-Mikoto|1=Izanami}} *{{ill|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime|Q1781862|qid=Q1781862|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime-no-Mikoto|1=Konohanasakuya-hime}} === Annual festival === 2nd Sunday of April. Nakatsugawa Taiko, Kamiyo-shishi, and others are dedicated. === History === This is Nakagawa Shrine, a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} of {{ill|lt=Ena District|Q3030924|qid=Q3030924|1=Ena District, Gifu}}, {{ill|lt=Mino Province|Q864156|qid=Q864156|1=Mino Province}}. It is the {{ill|lt=ubusunagami|Q1392097|qid=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} of Nakatsugawa City. Founding date is unknown. Since {{ill|lt=Mount Ena|Q1339920|qid=Q1339920|1=Mount Ena}} is regarded as the place where {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Amaterasu|1=UNKNOWN}}'s placenta was placed ({{ill|lt=Ena Shrine|Q11492453|qid=Q11492453|1=Ena Shrine (Nakatsugawa)}}), this area is thought to have long been sacred, and there is also a tradition that {{ill|lt=Izanagi|Q813858|qid=Q813858|lt=Izanagi-no-Mikoto|1=Izanagi}} was born here. Formerly it was called Hakusan-sha or Susukinomori. One theory says it began with worship of a goddess of the {{ill|lt=Kiso River|Q779607|qid=Q779607|1=Kiso River}}, and that this deity was later changed into three goddess deities (Kukuri-hime, Izanami, Konohanasakuya-hime). The current reading is "Nakagawa," but in older times it was also read "Nakawa" and "Nakatsukawa," and one theory says this gave rise to the place name Nakatsugawa. Also, the {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa River (Gifu)|Q11366440|qid=Q11366440|lt=Nakatsugawa|1=Nakatsu River}}, a tributary of the Kiso River, was formerly called Nakagawa. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki" and "Nakagawa Kyūki," it was originally the main Kumano Gongen shrine, and at rebuilding Hakusan Daigongen (Kukuri-hime) was made principal while Kumano (Izanami) and Fuji (Konohanasakuya-hime) were enshrined in the aiden; a {{ill|lt=bettōji|Q11396755|qid=Q11396755|1=Bettō-ji}} called '''Taishō-ji''' managed the shrine. The temple site is on the southern foot of Nakagawa Shrine. It is now a residential area, but one hōkyōintō and one gorintō remain. White porcelain-like sherds (yamachawan) were collected from a garden on the ridge connected to Nakagawa Shrine, but it is unclear whether they indicate kiln remains or a dwelling site. According to "Mino Kodō Misakagoe-ki," in {{ill|lt=Tenshō|Q1145058|qid=Q1145058|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1574|Q6680|qid=Q6680|1=1574}}), Hakusan Daigongen was burned by war during {{ill|lt=Takeda Katsuyori|Q662482|qid=Q662482|1=Takeda Katsuyori}}'s invasion of {{ill|lt=Tōnō|Q7862707|qid=Q7862707|1=Tōnō}}. At that time the middle seat was Hakusan, left Kumano, right Fuji; the ornate shrine and monks' quarters were burned, and only graves of the priest and shrine monks remained. It was rebuilt in {{ill|lt=Enpō|Q1145735|qid=Q1145735|1=Enpō}} 5 ({{ill|lt=1677|Q7631|qid=Q7631|1=1677}}). Possibly because of this, in the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}} only three shrines are seen for {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa-juku|Q2420820|qid=Q2420820|1=Nakatsugawa-juku}} on the {{ill|lt=Nakasendō|Q1154479|qid=Q1154479|1=Nakasendō}} (Hachiman shrine, Hakusan Gongen shrine, Shinmei shrine), and the name Nakagawa Shrine disappears. It is thought to have been divided and re-enshrined sometime between the Sengoku-period destruction and the early Edo period. In the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|qid=Q3125096}}, the "{{ill|lt=Hakusan Gongen|Q11579578|qid=Q11579578|lt=Hakusan Gongen shrine|1=Hakusan Gongen}}" came to be treated as Nakagawa Shrine, and in {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|qid=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} 6 ({{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}}) it became a village shrine. The current honden is the rebuild of Enpō 5 (1677), but the haiden and related buildings were not rebuilt then. In {{ill|lt=Heisei|Q234510|qid=Q234510|1=Heisei era}} 14 ({{ill|lt=2002|Q1987|qid=Q1987|1=2002}}), the haiden and naoraiden were rebuilt and the precinct was also improved. === Access === *About 800 m from {{ill|lt=Nakatsugawa Station|Q5359511|qid=Q5359511|1=Nakatsugawa Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Chūō Main Line|Q1078110|qid=Q1078110|1=Chūō Main Line}} === Other === *Directly beneath Nakagawa Shrine precinct runs an uphill-line {{ill|lt=tunnel|Q44377|qid=Q44377|1=Tunnel}} of the Chūō Main Line. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, I. Ancient Shrines, pp.1583-1587, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Nakatsugawa City History,' Middle Vol. II, Part 5 Early Modern (2), Ch.8 Temples and Shrines, Sec.1 Shrines, II. Medieval Shrines, pp.1587-1592, Nakatsugawa City, 1988 * 'Ena District History,' Part 3 Heian Period (later ancient period), Ch.12 Shrines and Buddhism, Nakagawa Shrine, p.74, Ena District Education Association, 1926 * 'Toki City History 1 (Prehistory - Battle of Sekigahara),' Part 12 Early Modern Feudal Society, Ch.1 Azuchi-Momoyama Period, "Burning of Kitano Hakusan Daigongen," p.397, Toki City History Editorial Committee, 1970 {{Shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Nakagawa Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Mino Province]] [[Category:Son-sha in Gifu Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nakatsugawa]]
